one_line_takeaway: The paper reframes LLM agent guardrails as control-theoretic safety filters computed in the agent's latent text representation -- jointly learning a safety monitor and a corrective recovery policy via safety-centric RL -- and shows in three simulated domains (driving, e-commerce budget, backseat driving advice) that this consequence-aware approach beats refusal-only baselines (zero-shot LLM judges, LlamaGuard, myopic reward-based recovery) on both safety and task success.
key_finding: In Agentic Driving (Table 1), ReGuard achieves 77.3% success / 18.8% failure with Value F1 0.99, versus e.g. GPT-5 (Think: minimal) at 39.5% success / 59.4% failure with Value F1 0.29, GPT-4o at 31.7%/68.2% (F1 0.29), Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct at 22.8%/76.8% (F1 0.0), and the Myopic-Realistic baseline at 44.4% success. The Privileged upper bound reaches 85.2% success / 13.2% failure. In Agentic Commerce (Table 2, budget=$50), ReGuard reaches 87.5% success, matching GPT-4o (87.5%) and beating zero-shot Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (62.5%), Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct (50%), and Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct (62.5%) -- the paper reports this as roughly a 25% improvement over the zero-shot Llama baselines. In the shielding experiments (Fig. 4), ReGuard has the highest success rate and rollout F1 across all three base models shielded (Llama-3.2-1B/3B/8B-Instruct); LlamaGuard shows the lowest failure rate only because its implicit policy is to halt the simulation entirely, which the authors argue is not a realistic option for embodied systems. In Backseat Driving (Tables 3-4), ReGuard improves success rate over the base Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct model from 82.4% to 84.9% (none persona) and 42.9% to 53.3% (urgent persona) in the Full setting, and from 44.9% to 64.2% (none) and 26.4% to 41.6% (urgent) in the Shielded setting using Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the shielded agent, with intervention rates of 62.1% and 72.4% respectively.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes generative AI (LLM agent) safety as a sequential decision problem within a POMDP, and derives 'predictive guardrails' as control-theoretic safety filters (a monitor + a recovery/fallback policy) computed directly in an LLM agent's latent text-based representation of the world, rather than in the raw physical state space. Concretely, it (1) defines a maximal latent safe set and safety value function via Hamilton-Jacobi-style reach-avoid analysis operating on the LLM's latent embedding of observations/actions, (2) gives a practical, scalable training recipe using a time-discounted safety Bellman equation trained with a DDQN-style safety-centric reinforcement learning procedure (fine-tuning Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct with LoRA) to jointly learn a safety monitor (Q-function) and a corrective fallback policy, and (3) at inference time blends the base LLM's token logits with the learned safety-value logits to steer generation away from unsafe actions in real time. The resulting guardrail ('ReGuard') is presented as model-agnostic: it can be wrapped around different, larger base LLM agents without access to their weights.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial/attacker threat model (no jailbreaks, prompt injection, or malicious users are considered). The 'threat' addressed is accidental/emergent unsafe behavior by an LLM agent acting autonomously or semi-autonomously in the world -- i.e., the agent's own outputs leading to downstream physical or financial harm (vehicle collisions, off-road excursions, exceeding a shopping budget) through the normal, non-adversarial course of task execution. The paper explicitly frames existing output-classification guardrails (e.g., LlamaGuard-style content filters) as brittle because they use textual proxies and one-step labels rather than modeling multi-step, real-world consequences, and because they only support refusal (blocking) with no path to safe recovery.
limitations: The authors state their own limitations explicitly: the approach assumes access to a simulator that provides reliable, ground-truth safety outcome signals during training, which is an open challenge for many real agentic domains where such simulators do not exist; and the guardrail is trained for a single fixed safety specification, with generalizing to test-time-specified or multi-stakeholder safety constraints left to future work. They also note no formal safety guarantees are provided (as with most deep-learning-based safety systems), though they point to conformal-prediction-based post-hoc guarantees as a possible extension. All experiments are in simulation (a 2D driving toy environment, a WebArena e-commerce sandbox, and a simulated human-proxy driver), so real-world / physical deployment validity is unestablished. The FAQ appendix also clarifies the driving scenario is a testbed, not a proposal to let LLMs directly drive real cars.

datasets_used: Custom Agentic Driving simulation (planar vehicle, obstacle avoidance, road boundaries)
WebArena-based Agentic Commerce simulation (shopping site accessibility tree, $50 budget constraint)
Custom Backseat AI Driver simulation with human-proxy policy (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) under 'none' and 'urgent' personas
quantitative_results: Agentic Driving: ReGuard 77.3% success / 18.8% failure / Value F1 0.99 vs Privileged 85.2%/13.2%/1.00, GPT-5(Think:minimal) 39.5%/59.4%/0.29, GPT-4o 31.7%/68.2%/0.29, GPT-4o-mini 23.4%/70.2%/0.29, Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct 22.8%/76.8%/0.0, Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct 22.3%/72.5%/0.78, Myopic-Privileged 81.9%/16.2%, Myopic-Realistic 44.4%/55.6%

Agentic Commerce success rate (8 initial cart conditions): ReGuard 87.5%, GPT-4o 87.5%, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct 62.5%, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct 50%, Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct 62.5% -- reported as ~25% improvement over zero-shot Llama baselines
Backseat Driving Full: none persona ReGuard 84.9%/15.1%/0.99 vs base Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct 82.4%/16.6%/0.97; urgent persona ReGuard 53.3%/46.5%/0.76 vs base 42.9%/56.7%/0.66

Backseat Driving Shielding: none persona +ReGuard 64.2%/25.4%/0.84 (62.1% intervention rate) vs Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct alone 44.9%/54.7%/0.68; urgent persona +ReGuard 41.6%/57.1%/0.65 (72.4% intervention rate) vs 26.4%/67.8%/0.47
baselines_compared: Privileged (upper-bound guardrail with true world state access)
Myopic-Privileged and Myopic-Realistic (PPO-trained recovery policies, supervised by privileged labels or LLM-as-a-judge reward)
Zero-shot foundation models as safety filters: GPT-5 (Think: minimal), GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct
LlamaGuard (retrained in-domain) as monitor-only baseline, and LlamaGuard+Myopic-Realistic / LlamaGuard+Myopic-Privileged as combined monitor+recovery baselines
Base (unshielded) LLM agents of varying size: Llama-3.2-1B/3B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
